source.body:
Stephanie Nguyen: for the NA pilot we need to lock “who owns what” before signals start spamming everyone 😅

Stephanie Nguyen: questions:
1) if company already has an AE owner in HubSpot → do we preserve?
2) if company is an existing customer w/ CSM assigned → does CSM get first look?
3) for net-new self-serve companies w/ no owner → round robin which pool?

Isabelle Laurent: +1. rule of thumb from Sales Ops: preserve existing owners unless we have explicit “reassign on X”. otherwise we create chaos.

David Kim: technically easiest too. router can: lookup company → if `hubspot_owner_id` present, keep it. else assign via workflow/rotation.

Avery Johnson: what about cases where the “owner” is an SDR from like 6 months ago who’s not on the account anymore?

Isabelle Laurent: good call. we should treat “inactive owner” as unowned. David can we detect via active user list?

David Kim: yep. we can validate owner id against active users. if inactive → route to pool.

Grace O'Connor: pls don’t route signal tasks to SDRs for this pilot unless we agree. we don’t have capacity for a new inbound stream without guardrails.

Stephanie Nguyen: agreed. pilot is AE-led, SDR optional later.

Ben Carter: from Growth side: we also need to not double-touch accounts that are already in a lifecycle email or in a nurture sequence.

Tyler Benson: marketing ops can add a suppression flag (e.g., “In PLG Nurture = true”) to block task creation or at least delay. but for ownership specifically: AE ownership should still be set even if task suppressed.

Jasmine Liu: CS angle: if it’s an existing paying customer (Hosted paid or Dedicated) and the signal looks like expansion, CSM needs to be looped in before outreach.

Markus Klein: +1. esp if signal is reliability (429s, latency) — could be support escalation not a sales moment. we should avoid “hey want to upgrade?” while they’re on fire.

Stephanie Nguyen: ok let’s define categories:
- Net-new self-serve (no contract): AE owns outreach.
- Existing customer: preserve AE owner if exists, but notify CSM + coordinate first.
- Support/p0 signals: route to CS/support first?

Jasmine Liu: we can tag “CS Notify Only” for certain signals (e.g., repeated 5xx, incident-related) vs “Sales Outreach OK”.

Ben Carter: PRD has separation between “threshold” vs “intent” signals. maybe we treat intent (pricing/security/dedicated docs) as safer for sales outreach, threshold-only as “check context”.

Isabelle Laurent: ownership rules proposal (NA pilot):
1) If company has active AE owner → preserve.
2) Else if company has CSM owner and `customer_status = paying` → preserve CSM owner + create task for AE? (or just notify AE?)
3) Else assign AE via round robin (NA Self-Serve AE pool).

Avery Johnson: wait if CSM is owner on an existing customer, should AE task still go to AE or to CSM?

Jasmine Liu: CSM should get a heads up but AE should own commercial convo. I’d prefer: task assigned to AE, and CSM added as follower / mention in note.

David Kim: HubSpot tasks don’t have followers per se, but router can:
- create task assigned to AE
- create timeline note @-mentioning CSM name (or include “CSM: X”)
- set a custom property like `plg_signal_last_notified_csm = true`

Isabelle Laurent: also we can add “CSM Name” property to company and include it in the task body.

Grace O'Connor: can we keep SDR out of it for now. if unowned → AE pool, not SDR pool.

Stephanie Nguyen: yes. AE-only pilot.

Tyler Benson: suppression logic suggestion:
- If `last_marketing_touch < 3 days` → delay task creation 48h (or create but with due date later)
- If “In outbound sequence” (sales sequence) → don’t create new task, just append note.

Ben Carter: +1 append note instead of new task if already being worked.

Markus Klein: SE involvement: if signal includes “Dedicated/Private docs viewed” or “VPC intent” → assign SE on creation? or at least tag for SE assist.

Stephanie Nguyen: tagging works. ownership stays AE but task template can say “loop SE if: VPC/private intent, compliance signals, repeated 429s + high spend velocity.”

Isabelle Laurent: round robin pool: NA pilot AEs = Avery J, 2 others? (I’ll DM list). should also map based on region? (US vs Canada?)

Stephanie Nguyen: keep it simple: NA = one pool for pilot. we can add region mapping in phase 2.

David Kim: implementation details:
- we’ll store `plg_signal_owner_reason` = existing_owner | existing_csm | round_robin
- `plg_signal_owner_id` set to final assignee
- `plg_signal_task_created` true/false
- also we should keep an audit note for any reassignment due to inactive owners.

Avery Johnson: can we add an “opt-out” / “don’t contact” check? don’t want SUP tickets.

Jasmine Liu: yes please. if customer has “Do not contact” or “Support-only” flag, no outreach.

Isabelle Laurent: HubSpot has “Communication subscription” + manual “Do not contact” property. we can gate on both.

Tyler Benson: + add internal/test org denylist so we don’t route Redwood demo accounts.

Ben Carter: agree. noise kills pilot.

Stephanie Nguyen: summarizing decisions (pls react if correct):
- Preserve existing active owner.
- If owner inactive → treat as unowned.
- For existing customers w/ CSM: task to AE, include CSM name + CS notify flag.
- Net-new self-serve: assign via AE round robin (no SDR pool).
- If already in active sales sequence: append note, don’t create additional task.
- Gate on DNC/opt-out + internal/test denylist.

Stephanie Nguyen: :thumbsup:?

Isabelle Laurent: :thumbsup:

David Kim: :thumbsup: I’ll update HubSpot property spec + confirm what we can check via API.

Jasmine Liu: :thumbsup: + can we also add a “CS heads up” slack ping later? (not for v1, but soon)

Markus Klein: :thumbsup: and please make the task template include “verify this isn’t a support escalation” before pitching Dedicated.

Grace O'Connor: :thumbsup: thanks for keeping SDRs out for now.

Tyler Benson: :thumbsup: I’ll align suppression flags with marketing so we don’t double-email.

Ben Carter: :thumbsup: will update playbook doc w/ the ownership + suppression rules so AEs know what to expect.

Maya Ortiz: Quick sync - Sylvan Health asked whether we can include per-token pricing tiers for POC (dev/test vs. preprod). They want a small cell in the RFI that shows 'unit price (dev)', 'unit price (preprod)', 'notes'.

Ben Carter: Recommend: dev/test = hosted trial rate $0.0006/token, preprod = discounted committed rate $0.00045/token for the POC term (3 months). Add note about reserved Dedicated cost separate.

Maya Ortiz: Also need a clarifying sentence about billing during fallback. PricingBot?

PricingBot: Billing note: "When controlled fallback routes traffic to hosted, hosted unit pricing applies for overflow tokens; Dedicated reserved capacity charges remain unchanged; egress and cross-region costs may apply per region."

Maya Ortiz: Great. Ops-bot, can you confirm if we have a pre-set POC billing cap template?

ops-bot: Yes, template available. Default cap: $25k/month for POC; can be adjusted with approval. Include link: https://docs.redwood.internal/poc-billing-cap-template :link:

Maya Ortiz: I'll add a short RFI line: 'POC billing cap available upon request; defaults to $25k/mo.' Will attach the billing cap form.

Ben Carter: Also, include in the RFI that hosted fallback will be billed at 'hosted rates' and that customers can request billing caps specifically for overflow traffic.

Maya Ortiz: Adding now. End.

source.text:
Avery Johnson: heads up scheduling conflict 😬 I’ve got a 10:00am PT demo tomorrow and just saw Hannah has one at the same time. Are we both using the demo tenant?

Hannah Schmitt: yep. mine is 10am PT too (EMEA team joining late). was planning to use the standard demo project + key.

Avery Johnson: if we share the same project/key we’re gonna step on each other’s rate limits + dashboards will look weird (traffic spikes / mixed traces)

Markus Klein: yeah, we can’t run two live demos against the same demo project. We’ve hit the “noisy neighbor” problem before (429s + confusing Console graphs).

Talia Benson: +1 please treat parallel demos as separate “namespaces” — that’s one of the requirements. What’s the fastest workaround for tomorrow?

Devon Price: workaround is: create two separate projects under the demo org, each with its own scoped key + quota caps.

Devon Price: we don’t have full “parallel demos” automation rolled out to everyone yet, but we can do it manually w/ the bootstrap CLI.

Avery Johnson: can someone do that today? I don’t have admin perms in the demo org.

Ethan Park: I can. confirming: you need isolated API keys and also isolated Console views (so separate project is best).

Hannah Schmitt: separate project works. I also want the “rollouts” view to look populated. will that still show w/ a separate project?

Allison Grant: yes, as long as the synthetic traffic job targets that project too. Otherwise you’ll get empty graphs.

Markus Klein: key point: do NOT paste keys in Slack. We’ll drop them in 1Password shared vault + send you item links.

Avery Johnson: :thumbsup: yep.

Devon Price: proposed naming:
- `demo-ae-avery-2025-05-21`
- `demo-ae-hannah-2025-05-21`
Each gets:
- 1 chat route pinned to `rw-llama-3.1-70b` (demo pin)
- fallback policy enabled (same as main demo)
- default rate limit 60 rpm / 200k tok/min (enough for live)

Sean Gallagher: + please set burst limit to avoid a runaway script nuking the pool. We’ve had a tab-refresh loop do this.

Ethan Park: ack. I’ll set:
- max_concurrent_requests=20
- 429 retry-after enabled
Also will add “reset safety” tag so no one wipes non-demo projects.

Hannah Schmitt: thank you. can we also get a separate “seeded dashboards” look? I need cost/latency charts to not be empty.

Allison Grant: I can point the traffic generator at both projects tonight. It can run per-project with a label filter.

Allison Grant: something like:
```
rw-demo trafficgen --org demo --project demo-ae-hannah-2025-05-21 --preset sales-standard --duration 45m
rw-demo trafficgen --org demo --project demo-ae-avery-2025-05-21 --preset sales-standard --duration 45m
```

Devon Price: for the bootstrap:
```
rw-demo bootstrap --org demo --project demo-ae-avery-2025-05-21 --preset sales-standard
rw-demo bootstrap --org demo --project demo-ae-hannah-2025-05-21 --preset sales-standard
```
It’s idempotent.

Avery Johnson: perfect. also can we keep the “demo app” config separate so I don’t accidentally point to Hannah’s project?

Markus Klein: yes. we’ll generate two keys:
- `DEMO_AVERY_SCOPED_KEY`
- `DEMO_HANNAH_SCOPED_KEY`
Both scoped to their project only.

Ethan Park: doing this now. ETA 20 min.

Talia Benson: action items:
1) Ethan creates two projects + scoped keys today
2) Allison seeds synthetic traffic to populate Console dashboards
3) Markus shares key links via 1Password + quick instructions
Please reply here when done so we can mark this as the temporary workaround.

Ethan Park: update: projects created. routes pinned + fallback enabled. quotas set. keys generated + stored in 1Password (shared “GTM Demo Tenant” vault).

Markus Klein: sent both of you the 1Password item links via DM. Reminder: use the project-specific Console URL so you don’t end up in the default demo project.

Allison Grant: trafficgen running for both projects now. dashboards should be non-empty within ~10 mins (ingestion lag).

Hannah Schmitt: got it. will do a quick dry run later today.

Avery Johnson: same. thanks all.

Sean Gallagher: if either of you see 429s tomorrow, ping #demo-oncall (not #sales) and include the healthcheck output:
```
rw-demo healthcheck --org demo --project <name>
```

Markus Klein: longer-term: we’re working on “parallel demos support” so this becomes self-serve. For now, if you schedule overlapping demos, drop the times in #sales at least 24h ahead so we can pre-provision.

Talia Benson: noted. closing loop on this thread — workaround confirmed for tomorrow.

source.body:
Avery Johnson: quick q before my 2pm — is it OK to show Audit Logs in the live demo? prospect asked about "who changed what". don’t want to trip security/compliance.

Markus Klein: yep we *can*, but only if we follow the safe-sharing rules. Naomi/Marcus can you confirm what’s allowed?

Naomi Feldman: Allowed, with guardrails. The short version:
- Only in the demo tenant (synthetic data).
- Don’t show secrets (API keys, bearer tokens), and avoid exposing full IPs / internal hostnames.
- Don’t click into any “raw event payload” that includes request bodies.
- Keep it to: event type, actor (role), resource type, timestamp, outcome.

Avery Johnson: got it. the UI has a “details” drawer w/ JSON. should i just not open that?

Marcus Lin: Correct — don’t open the raw JSON/event payload during an external call. That view can include internal IDs and occasionally request metadata we don’t want to explain.

Markus Klein: +1. For the live path: show the table view only, then talk track the rest.

Avery Johnson: what about showing that SSO user did an action? like “Okta / SAML user X changed RBAC role”

Naomi Feldman: That’s fine if the user is synthetic + generic. Use “Demo Admin” / “SE Operator” style names. No real employee emails.

Talia Benson: FYI the demo seed has a couple fake identities already — “demo-admin@redwood.example” etc. Please don’t create new ones w/ real domains.

Avery Johnson: makes sense. any specific things that are an absolute no?

Marcus Lin: Hard no’s for external demos:
- Any customer-identifiable info (names, emails, company domains)
- API keys / session tokens (even briefly)
- Internal Slack/Jira links from the audit log UI
- Anything that reveals infrastructure topology (private hostnames, cluster IDs)
- Raw request/response bodies (prompt text, tool payloads)

Markus Klein: also avoid the “export CSV” button. it downloads w/ internal fields and people get weirdly focused on it.

Avery Johnson: lol noted.

Elliot Price: We have a canned snippet in the Security FAQ for exactly this question (“Can you provide audit logs?”). You can quote it + then show the safe view. Internal link: confluence → Sales Enablement → Security FAQ → “Audit logs” section.

Avery Johnson: perfect. what’s the best framing line?

Naomi Feldman: Suggested phrasing (safe + accurate):
“Redwood records control-plane actions in an immutable audit trail — things like key creation, role changes, routing policy edits, and login events. In production you can export these to your SIEM, and we support retention controls. For the demo, we’re showing synthetic events only and we’ll keep the view high-level.”

Markus Klein: If they ask “can I see request-level logs?” the answer is: audit logs are for *admin actions*, not prompt/response content. Then pivot to observability/traces in Console (also synthetic).

Avery Johnson: in the UI the audit log list is empty sometimes. what do i do if it’s blank?

Markus Klein: Run the “seed-audit-events” step OR just trigger one safe action live:
- rotate a demo API key (in demo project)
- or toggle a routing policy flag
Those generate clean audit events.

Talia Benson: pls don’t rotate keys unless you’re in the “Avery-*” demo namespace project. We had a collision last week w/ parallel demos.

Avery Johnson: ok. can you drop the exact do/don’t checklist i can paste into my prep notes?

Marcus Lin: Do:
- Confirm you’re in Demo Org + correct project
- Filter time range to “Last 24h” and resource type “Access / RBAC / Keys”
- Show 3–5 events max (role change, key created, policy updated)
- If an IP is visible, keep it at /24 masked or don’t mention it
Don’t:
- Open event JSON
- Click “export”
- Show user profile pages
- Scroll back weeks/months (risk of old test data w/ weird fields)

Naomi Feldman: and if you need a backup: use the approved screenshot set (Design made annotated ones). Ask Markus for the latest.

Markus Klein: I’ll DM you the backup screenshots + the “safe audit log view” bookmark. :thumbsup:

Avery Johnson: awesome, thanks all. :pray: :lock:

Elliot Price: I’ll update the demo scenario checklist with “Audit log safe mode” notes (table view only, no JSON).

source.thread:
Avery Johnson: on the live call rn — Console dashboards are totally empty 😬 cost/latency/errors all zero. using demo tenant “redwood-demo”. anyone know the quickest fix?

Markus Klein: ugh. which project are you in? also what time range?

Avery Johnson: project: `demo-core-api` (i think). time range last 1h. nothing. even “requests” graph blank.

Dylan Brooks: if it’s that blank, ingestion isn’t getting events OR you’re in the wrong project. try switching to `demo-prod-econ` and set time range to 24h.

Avery Johnson: switched + 24h. still empty.

Allison Grant: ok this is the “seed job didn’t run” failure mode. workaround: kick synthetic traffic generator.

Markus Klein: +1. we’ve been doing this before demos if dashboards look cold.

Avery Johnson: how do i run it while on the call? i’m screensharing.

Allison Grant: don’t run it on screen. tell them “data is anonymized + synthetic, we’ll refresh the dashboard feed for the demo tenant” and go to backup screenshots slide for 2 mins.

Elliot Price: backup screenshots are in the deck appendix (“Console tour (backup)”) — slide has the pre-captured graphs.

Avery Johnson: ok i can pivot. but i still need the graphs to populate for the second half.

Mei Lin: you can trigger it via the internal job runner. command:
`rdwd demo traffic start --preset console_baseline --org redwood-demo --project demo-prod-econ --duration 6m`
should start showing within ~60–90s.

Markus Klein: ^ that’s the right one. also if you have the SE toolbox installed:
`rdwd demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo`
it’ll tell you if telemetry pipe is down vs just no traffic.

Avery Johnson: i have the CLI. running now (off screen).

Redwood Deploy Bot: Job queued: `demo-synth-traffic` (preset=console_baseline, org=redwood-demo, project=demo-prod-econ)

Sean Gallagher: if it doesn’t show up after 2 mins, try swapping region in Console top-right (sometimes you’re viewing EU while traffic is in us-east) + hard refresh.

Avery Johnson: good call. i was set to EU (why??). switched to us-east + refresh.

Avery Johnson: graphs are starting to fill 🙏 i see requests + p95 latency now.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: once you have some data, keep the range at 24h or 6h so it doesn’t look “spiky empty” if the generator stops.

Markus Klein: can someone take ownership to make this not happen mid-demo? this thread keeps repeating.

Dylan Brooks: agreed. root cause likely seed schedule + Console default project selection. Mei/Logan can we make the traffic generator run every X mins for demo org + alert if dashboard freshness goes stale?

Mei Lin: I can own the seed schedule + job reliability. I’ll file a ticket: “demo dashboards cold start / seed job guardrail”. also will add a cron to keep baseline traffic during business hours.

Allison Grant: I’ll add an alert on “demo dashboard freshness” and wire to #sales-demo-oncall (or whatever alias we’re using). Sean ok with that?

Sean Gallagher: yes. and let’s update the pre-demo checklist: run `rdwd demo healthcheck` 10 mins before call. if it fails, run the seed job and confirm graphs.

Elliot Price: I’ll add that to the demo scenario checklist + runbook, plus a 30-second “what to say when dashboards are empty” snippet.

Avery Johnson: call saved. thanks all — back to live demo now. please keep this thread link handy for the next poor soul 😅

Markus Klein: leaving this pinned in #sales for now. and Avery — after the call, can you DM me which Console URL you were on + region? helps for debugging.

Avery Johnson: yep will do.

Markus Klein: heads up — demo reset is still taking *forever*.

I just did it before a 2pm and it was ~18 min end-to-end and I had no idea if it was stuck or just slow. Can we please get to a *single command* reset + a clear healthcheck output? Right now it’s like 5 steps + Console still shows empty graphs half the time.

(reaction: :sweat_smile:)

Avery Johnson: +1. I’m sitting on calls like “one sec while we reset…” and it’s brutal. Even a “good / degraded / broken” readout would help.

Devon Price: yep, heard. Can you paste what you ran + where it spent time? Is it provisioning or the seed job?

Markus Klein: rough sequence:
1) rotate key (manual)
2) run bootstrap script
3) run seed traffic
4) wait for ingestion
5) click around Console praying it’s not empty

The long pole is waiting for “dashboards to look alive”. Also the bootstrap script logs are noisy — hard to tell success vs partial.

Sean Gallagher: we should define what “reset complete” means (SLO wise) and bake that into the command output. Right now we’re optimizing for the wrong thing (script finished != demo ready).

Ethan Park: on the auth/key rotation piece — we can bundle that into the reset if we keep it scoped to demo org + guardrails. But we need to be careful about not nuking non-demo tenants.

Chloe Martin: Console side: empty graphs usually means either (a) wrong project selected, (b) default time range mismatch, or (c) seed events not arriving. We can make the healthcheck verify ingestion and preselect the seeded project.

Allison Grant: + can add a “dashboard freshness” check: last telemetry event timestamp per project. If it’s > N minutes old, healthcheck should fail and tell you what to run.

Talia Benson: can we align on the ask as:
- `demo reset` is idempotent, safe, and < 5 min
- `demo healthcheck` gives a single pass/fail + actionable next steps
- includes a “ready for live demo” checklist

Devon Price: proposing:
`rw-demo reset --tenant demo --profile se-default`
Under the hood:
- validates org/tenant is demo
- rotates scoped key (optional `--rotate-key` default on)
- runs bootstrap (projects/routes/prompt sets)
- kicks off synthetic traffic generator (10–20 req/s for 3–5 min)
- waits for ingestion signal and then prints status

Output something like:
```
DEMO RESET SUMMARY
Tenant: redwood-demo (org_8f3…)
Project: demo-core (proj_12a…)
Model pin: redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct@2026-02-01 ✅
Fallback policy: family=llama-3.1, region=us-east->us-west ✅
Quota: rpm=600 / tpm=240k ✅
Synthetic traffic: running (job_7c1…) ✅
Telemetry ingestion: last event 23s ago ✅
Console dashboards: ready (freshness < 2m) ✅
Overall: READY
```

Markus Klein: YES. Also please make it scream when something’s wrong. Like not just “failed” but “quota too low (currently 60 rpm), run X or ping Y”.

Sean Gallagher: +1. And include escalation contact in output. In the moment, SEs need the “who do I page” line.

Allison Grant: we can print:
- Slack channel: `#demo-oncall`
- runbook link
- last 5 errors (if any)

Also if we detect 429s in the last 5 min, we should warn before you start the live demo.

Ethan Park: guardrails suggestion:
- require `--i-understand-this-resets-demo-data` or a typed confirmation unless `--non-interactive` used by CI
- hardcode allowlist of demo org IDs
- rate limit resets (we’ve seen people spam it during panic)

Avery Johnson: can we also have a “fast mode” for back-to-back demos? Like don’t rebuild everything, just rotate key + restart traffic job.

Devon Price: yep, we can do `rw-demo reset --fast` (skip bootstrap unless drift detected). Healthcheck can detect drift (missing route/model pin mismatch) and force full reset.

Chloe Martin: if we have the healthcheck write the “expected project” into the Console deep link, that will eliminate half the “empty dashboard” reports.

Talia Benson: next steps:
- Devon: spec + ETA for `rw-demo reset` + `rw-demo healthcheck`
- Chloe/Allison: define ingestion/freshness signals and where to fetch them
- Sean: define demo-ready criteria + error budget guidance

Markus Klein: thank you. If we can get this before next week’s AE training, it’ll save us.

DemoOps Bot: reminder: demo environment incidents/escalations → #demo-oncall. If live demo in <60 min and blocked, tag @sean.gallagher + @devon.price.

source.content:
Avery Johnson: Deal blocker 🚨 QuantGrid procurement is holding signature until we commit (in writing) to a “Runtime LTS channel” w/ support window + patch cadence. They got burned by a latency regression on a prior vendor and want explicit stability + backport policy. Can someone from Release Eng/Runtime give me approved wording + timeline we can stand behind?

Hannah Schmitt: +1, I’m seeing similar language pop up in NorthStar Bank security addendum. They’re asking “6–12 month stable baseline, security fixes, reproducible artifacts.” Need consistent answer.

Connor O'Brien: looping in @Mateo Alvarez + @Marissa Cole + @Rachel Kim. Avery — what are they asking for specifically (support duration, patch SLA, etc.)? Don’t want Sales committing to exact dates we can’t guarantee.

Avery Johnson: Their redlines (summary):
- LTS channel available for Dedicated + Private
- Support window >= 12 months per LTS major/minor baseline
- Monthly patch cadence, plus out-of-band security patches
- Security patch turnaround “within 72 hours of disclosure” (their words)
- Ability to pin version + get checksums/provenance
- “No breaking changes within support window”
They’re okay that new features don’t land on LTS.

Mateo Alvarez: We can commit to the *program*, but careful on the “72 hours of disclosure” part — depends on severity + reproducibility. We should phrase as target SLA for Critical/High with best-effort + customer notification.

Marissa Cole: Product POV: we’re positioning LTS as “slower-change, predictable patches” with explicit backport eligibility (security + Sev1/Sev2). Not “no changes,” but “no feature churn / controlled diffs.”

Rachel Kim: From CS: QuantGrid is a good pilot candidate, but procurement wants GA language. We can offer an addendum that references the public doc once published. They mainly need confidence we won’t force emergency upgrades.

Connor O'Brien: Current internal plan (what I’m comfortable putting in customer-facing language):
- First LTS line is runtime 1.18 LTS (tagged as `1.18.x-lts`)
- Support window: 12 months from GA of that LTS line
- Patch cadence: monthly (target), plus out-of-band for security + critical Sev1
- Backports: security + Sev1 + select Sev2 only; no new features
- Artifacts: signed/checksummed + traceable to commit + release notes; pinning supported in Private installer + Helm
Timeline: we’re in pilot phase now; aiming to GA the channel after pilot criteria are met. I’d rather not put a hard calendar date in the contract.

Dev Patel: Support angle: if they want “request a backport” language, we should say Support will triage Sev and route to Release Eng; acceptance per backport rubric; comms via ticket + release notes.

Avery Johnson: This is helpful. Can we give them *some* timeframe? They’re pushing for “available by end of Q2.”

Mateo Alvarez: I’m not comfortable committing to end-of-Q2 in contract language. We can say “GA planned in 2025” or “GA after successful pilot with enterprise customers.” If you need a date for deal motion, we can do a side letter w/ non-binding target.

Soojin Lee: Please avoid “guarantee” / “no breaking changes” phrasing. Recommended framing:
- “stability-focused channel”
- “backwards-compatibility is a priority; changes are communicated in LTS release notes”
- “exceptions may apply for critical security fixes”
Happy to review the exact paragraph before it goes out.

Hannah Schmitt: +1. Also they’ll ask what “supported” means (bugfixes only vs troubleshooting). We should reference Support tier separately.

Connor O'Brien: Proposed approved blurb (Sales can paste; tweak customer name only):
“Redwood Inference provides a stability-focused Runtime Long Term Support (LTS) channel intended for Dedicated and Private deployments. Each Runtime LTS line offers a stable baseline supported for 12 months from GA. Redwood targets a monthly patch cadence for LTS, and may issue out-of-band patches for security vulnerabilities and critical production defects. LTS patches prioritize minimal, reviewable changes and include ‘slower-change’ release notes (categorized changes, known issues/workarounds, and compatibility/upgrade guidance). Backports into an LTS line are limited to security fixes and high-severity production issues (Sev1 and select Sev2) subject to Redwood’s backport review and test/performance gates; new features are delivered in the standard release channel. Redwood also provides verifiable artifacts (checksums/signing/provenance) and supports version pinning for Private installer and Helm-based deployments.”

Connor O'Brien: For the security timing part: “For critical security issues, Redwood will prioritize remediation and communicate timelines; patch timing depends on severity and verification.” We should not accept “72 hours of disclosure” verbatim.

Avery Johnson: This is exactly what I need. Can you also give me one sentence about perf regressions (they’re obsessed with p95)?

Mateo Alvarez: Add: “LTS patches are gated by regression tests and performance benchmarks; if a patch introduces a material regression, Redwood will provide rollback guidance and/or a follow-up patch.” Don’t promise zero regressions.

Marissa Cole: + include “known issues” section in every LTS note so procurement sees we’re transparent.

Soojin Lee: If you send me the final paragraph you plan to email, I’ll do a quick wording pass today.

Avery Johnson: Will do. Connor — can you DM me this blurb + I’ll fold into the procurement response.

Connor O'Brien: DMing now + will also drop a short version for exec summary.

Rachel Kim: FYI if QuantGrid wants a pilot letter, we can offer “early access to LTS channel during pilot, with weekly check-ins” (non-contractual) — sometimes that unblocks.

Dev Patel: And if they ask for a backport request process, point them to Support ticket intake + required repro/logs + severity mapping.

Avery Johnson: Got it. Thanks all — I’ll push back on the 72h clause and use the “prioritize + communicate timelines” language. :thumbsup:

Soojin Lee: Please tag me on the email draft before it goes to procurement.

Connor O'Brien: Also: make sure they understand LTS is for runtime (kernels/scheduler/serving), not model behavior guarantees.

Avery Johnson: Good call — they conflated runtime vs model once already. I’ll clarify.

Hannah Schmitt: Following — would love to reuse the same blurb for NorthStar.

Connor O'Brien: Yep, treat it as standard. If Legal needs a reference, we’ll point them to the LTS policy doc once it’s published in customer-facing docs.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. Let’s keep all deal language consistent. If any customer pushes for hard SLAs, route through Exec + Legal.

Avery Johnson: Understood. Sending draft to @Soojin Lee in 10.

Soojin Lee: Standing by.

Stephanie Nguyen: Dropping this here because it keeps coming up on calls. We need the Dedicated tier cheat sheet to be *way* clearer on what we can promise in writing and how to explain burst vs committed throughput. Right now AEs are riffing.

Isabelle Laurent: +1. Got asked today: “If I buy Plus, do I always get X tokens/sec? Even if I change models? What if I spike traffic?” We need a crisp answer + a one-liner we can say without caveats spiraling.

Ava Chen: We’re updating enablement docs this week. Can folks paste the exact objections/questions they’re getting? I’ll consolidate and we’ll get Product/Finance to sign off on the talk track.

Stephanie Nguyen: Common ones:
1) “Is the throughput commitment a *minimum* or just an estimate?”
2) “Does burst mean free extra capacity or paid?”
3) “If we exceed the committed, will Redwood throttle us or just bill us?”
4) “Can I point to the SLO language as a guarantee for procurement?”
5) “What happens if you miss the commitment?”

Markus Klein: From Solutions side, also getting confusion on *where* it applies.
- Is it per deployment_id or per org?
- If customer has 2 models under one Dedicated deployment, does commitment split?
- Can they do long-context prompts and still expect same tps?

Isabelle Laurent: Another: customer asks “commit is 2k tok/s, but we’re streaming—how do you measure tokens/sec? Our UI shows different numbers than your dashboard.”

Lauren Bishop: Yep, we should tighten language. Quick draft talk track (happy to refine):
- **Committed throughput** = the floor Redwood commits to provide for the deployment under stated conditions (pinned model/version, supported context window, within configured concurrency / request shape). This is what the SLO is measured against.
- **Burst** = ability to temporarily exceed committed throughput when spare capacity exists. Not guaranteed; it’s “best effort.” How it’s billed depends on tier/contract (Standard: billed as overage; Plus: limited included burst; Premium: reserved headroom + higher likelihood of burst availability).

Lauren Bishop: Re: per deployment vs per org — commitment is **per Dedicated deployment** (deployment_id), not pooled across different deployments unless explicitly contracted as a multi-deployment bundle.

Soojin Lee: For cheat sheet, I’d recommend we standardize on phrases:
- “Guaranteed floor” for committed
- “Best-effort headroom” for burst
- “Measured at the Redwood edge” for the metric source
We should avoid “unlimited” and avoid implying burst is always available.

Laura Bennett: Finance note: please do not say “burst is free” unless you’re looking at a contract that explicitly includes it. Default posture we’ve been modeling:
- Standard: burst billed (metered separately)
- Plus: small included burst pool, then billed
- Premium: larger included burst + priority access, then billed
If you need exceptions, send to Deal Desk.

Stephanie Nguyen: Can we get an approved one-liner for procurement folks?
Like: “Redwood guarantees a minimum throughput of X tok/s for your Dedicated deployment, measured over Y minutes, excluding customer-side throttling / unsupported request shapes.”

Lauren Bishop: Yes. Here’s the **customer-facing version** I want in the cheat sheet (short form):
“**Committed Throughput**: Redwood will provide at least the committed tokens/sec for your Dedicated deployment when using the contracted model/version and supported context limits, measured over a rolling window. If Redwood fails to meet the commitment, you’re eligible for service credits per the order form.”

Rachel Kim: CS request: please add a simple “What to do if they claim they’re below commit” box.
- Ask for deployment_id + region
- Confirm model/version and whether they changed anything
- Point them to the Console widget (once live) OR ask for their request logs
- Route to Support with “Dedicated Throughput SLO” tag

Isabelle Laurent: We still need a clean answer on: “If I exceed committed, do you throttle me?” because customers assume Dedicated = infinite.

Lauren Bishop: Proposed answer:
- We won’t *silently* throttle committed traffic below the floor (that’s the point of the tier).
- For traffic above committed: depends on tier + saturation. We may admit, queue, or shed above-commit traffic to protect the floor and other customers. If it’s admitted, it may be billed as burst/overage.
We should say “admission control may apply above committed throughput.”

Markus Klein: + please call out that long-context can reduce tok/s. Customers do a 128k prompt and then ask why tok/s dipped.

Lauren Bishop: Agree. Add to cheat sheet under “Conditions / exclusions”:
- Model + version pinned (no unilateral swaps)
- Context window within supported max for the shape
- Request pattern within configured limits (e.g., max concurrent requests, max input length)
- Excludes customer-induced backpressure (client aborts, low read rate on streaming, their own throttles)

Isabelle Laurent: That streaming backpressure bit is huge. We need wording that doesn’t sound accusatory. Maybe: “Streaming throughput depends on client read rate; slow consumers can reduce observed tok/s.”

Soojin Lee: Copy suggestion for seller-friendly tone:
“Streaming performance reflects end-to-end delivery. If the client reads slowly, the system may pace output accordingly.”

Ava Chen: Action items I’m hearing:
1) Add “What you can promise” section with approved language (committed vs burst)
2) Add “What you can’t promise” bullets (model swaps, arbitrary long context, unlimited burst)
3) Add objection-handling Q&A for the 5 questions Steph listed
4) Add Deal Desk guardrails for “free burst” / discounting

Laura Bennett: Please include guardrails:
- Any language implying “unlimited burst” or “no overages ever” = Deal Desk
- Any discount > X% off list on Premium shapes = Deal Desk
(We can fill in the % once Sales Ops confirms current policy.)

Isabelle Laurent: Can we also include a simple example?
Like: “Commit 2k tok/s, you’re running 1.5k sustained = in compliance. You spike to 3k for 10 minutes, we may serve it as burst; not guaranteed; billed depending on contract.”

Lauren Bishop: Yes. And a “miss” example too:
“If measured throughput is < commit over the rolling window (and conditions met), it counts toward SLO error budget and triggers escalation/credits.”

Rachel Kim: +1 on including the escalation path and expectation-setting:
Premium: faster response target, Plus: standard, Standard: best effort (within support policy). Keep it consistent with actual CS ops.

Stephanie Nguyen: Can someone drop the link to the current cheat sheet doc? I might be looking at an older version.

Ava Chen: Current draft is in the enablement folder (internal): drive://shared_drives/go-to-market/dedicated/pricing/DEDICATED_TIER_CHEAT_SHEET_v1
I’ll post the updated v2 in this thread for final review.

Isabelle Laurent: Also can we explicitly answer “is the commitment tokens/sec or requests/sec?”

Lauren Bishop: We should standardize: tokens/sec as the canonical commitment for gen endpoints, with a note that for some workloads we may contract in requests/sec when request sizes are bounded. For the cheat sheet: “committed throughput is expressed in tokens/sec unless otherwise stated in the order form.”

Markus Klein: :thumbsup: Works. Just make sure AEs have the “unless otherwise stated” line so we don’t get boxed in.

Soojin Lee: I’ll rewrite the “burst” section to remove ambiguous phrases like “extra capacity included” and replace with “included burst allowance (if any) is specified in the order form.”

Laura Bennett: Thank you. Also please keep “list price guidance” out of the cheat sheet version that goes to all AEs if we’re worried about leaking—Sales Ops can host guardrails separately.

Isabelle Laurent: We need something though; people are quoting random overage rates. Maybe we keep it as “overages apply; ask Deal Desk for rate card”?

Stephanie Nguyen: +1. At least a “don’t invent numbers” warning.

Ava Chen: Noted. I’ll add a bright-red callout:
“Do not quote burst/overage rates from memory. Use the rate card / Deal Desk.”

Lauren Bishop: I’ll also add a crisp “Can I promise this in writing?” line:
- Standard: SLO language is in product terms, not always in the order form
- Plus/Premium: written commitment in order form (subject to conditions)
We can confirm exact contractual packaging with Sofia (Legal) separately.

Stephanie Nguyen: Perfect. Can we aim to lock v2 by EOW? I have 3 deals in procurement right now and this would help.

Ava Chen: Yes. I’ll circulate v2 tomorrow noon PT for async comments, final by Friday.

Isabelle Laurent: :raised_hands: thanks all. Please make the burst explanation idiot-proof (for me).

Ava Chen: lol noted. Keeping it 2 sentences max.

Lauren Bishop: Final proposed 2-sentence burst definition for the cheat sheet:
“**Burst** is throughput above your committed floor that Redwood may provide when capacity is available. Burst is not guaranteed and may be metered/billed per your order form.”

Stephanie Nguyen: Ship it.

Ava Chen: Cool. I’ll incorporate and repost for reactions/approval.

source.text:
Sam Wu: @channel OrionX RFI landed — they want a formal capability matrix, security answers, and a POC plan. PDF attached here: https://s3.redwood.internal/orionx_rfi_v1.pdf. Main asks: reserved capacity pricing vs burst, on-prem HSM option, timeline for a 3-week POC, SLA expectations. Who can drive technical answers?

Ava Chen: I can drive the capability matrix + POC ops plan. Can someone pull their security questionnaire into a spreadsheet so Legal can triage? Also need to confirm whether they require FIPS 140-2 L3 for HSM.

Ravi Kumar: Re: pricing — for demos we usually give a staged quote: 1) Proof-of-value (3w) = small reserved pool w/ burst enabled, 2) Prod reserved = monthly commit. I'll attach a short sample rate-card snippet. Note: internal guidance only, do not share verbatim with customer.

Leah Summers: Sam — can you confirm if the RFI wants RAG (retrieval) support listed as a separate capability row? The PDF's matrix is vague. Also they asked about per-route latency SLOs; we should surface Dedicated vs Hosted differences.

Sam Wu: Good catch. They explicitly asked for "documented per-route latency SLOs" and "prefix KV caching". I'll request a clarification but we should draft both variants.

Ava Chen: Draft capability matrix (first pass) below — use these rows for the CSV: feature,status,notes
```
Feature,Redwood (Hosted),Redwood (Dedicated),Redwood (Private)
Per-route latency SLOs,Offered / SLA-backed,Offered / SLA-backed,Customer-controlled
KV/prefix caching,Enabled (shared cache),Enabled (isolated per-tenant),Configured in-customer-network
Model rollouts & canary,Yes,Yes,Yes
Function/tool calling,Yes,Yes,Yes
Customer-managed KMS,No (managed),Yes (integrations),Yes (bring-your-own)
On-prem HSM support,No,Optional via Dedicated add-on,Available for Private with HSM appliance
Autoscaling / burst,Auto-burst,Configurable autoscale,Based on infra
```

Ava Chen: I'll convert to their CSV format and attach.

Ravi Kumar: For the sample rate-card snippet (placeholder):
```
POC (3 weeks) — reserved mini-pool: $N/month (pro-rated), burst tokens billed at $X per 1M tokens
Dedicated (monthly commit) — base price per GPU-equivalent: $Y/month + support
Private (on-prem) — licensing + install + support fees (one-time + recurring)
```

Ravi Kumar: Also note network egress & private connectivity are quoted separately.

Emily H: Legal note — do not promise HSM appliance specifics until we confirm partner hardware compatibility and export controls. If they return the security questionnaire, send it to me and I'll mark any risky answers. Also confirm whether they need SOC2 + FedRAMP language in the RFI.

Leah Summers: Quick ask for Solutions: can someone confirm whether our KV cache allows per-customer TTL config? I think yes for Dedicated/Private. If yes, add a note.

Ava Chen: Confirmed for Dedicated/Private: per-tenant TTL and eviction policies are configurable. Hosted supports configurable TTL up to a platform default. Adding note.

Sam Wu: I'm going to ask OrionX for clarification on HSM level (FIPS L2 vs L3) and whether they require an on-prem appliance. Meanwhile, Ava and Ravi please assemble the CSV + rate-card placeholders. Emily — I'll upload the security questionnaire spreadsheet when they send it.

deploy-bot: File uploaded: /attachments/orionx_rfi_questions.xlsx (uploader: Sam Wu)

Ava Chen: Uploaded draft CSV to the thread: https://s3.redwood.internal/drafts/orionx_capability_matrix.csv — please review.

Ravi Kumar: Added the pricing placeholders to the shared doc. Tagging @Sam Wu for review before we send.

Emily H: Received — will review security Qs when they populate. Heads-up: any mention of key export/import needs to be redlined.

Sam Wu: Thanks team — plan: compile answers by EOD tomorrow, run by Legal, then submit RFI bundle. If OrionX pushes for POC this week, we can compress to 2w but need approvals (ops + exec).

Leah Summers: Noted. I'll prepare a one-page POC acceptance criteria doc (data needed, success metrics, test prompts) and drop it here.

Ava Chen: I'll prepare the canary/fallback notes (how we handle model fallback to cheaper variants if capacity constrained) since they asked about graceful degradation.

Sam Wu: Great. Closing loop — I'll post final submission draft here before sending. :thumbsup:

Ethan Park: Morning everyone  first week in CS. Need HubSpot + Gong + Notion Sales Playbook. Also can't find the 'Enterprise one-pager' or demo sandbox creds. Who should I ping? :wave:
Nima (Sales Ops): Welcome Ethan! :tada: Quick steps: 1) fill access form https://forms.redwood.com/sales-access 2) reply here with your preferred email 3) we'll assign HubSpot seat & Notion. I can request Gong after.
Ethan Park: Done — ethan.park@redwood.com. Submitted form just now. Also want transcripts on for Gong if possible.
IT Helpdesk: Okta invite sent to ethan.park@redwood.com. If you don't get it check spam. To re-send run:
```
okta invite resend --email ethan.park@redwood.com
```
Access Bot: Created access ticket #SALES-7001 (HubSpot, Gong, Notion, Drive:Sales/Collateral). Track: https://redwood.atlassian.com/browse/SALES-7001
Zoe Martinez: Hi Ethan, I'm your CS buddy. For enterprise one-pager use Drive -> Sales -> Collateral -> Enterprise-OnePager.pdf. If you can't access, tag me and I'll share via 1Password. For demo creds, we use the sandbox in 1Password: "Sales / Sandboxes / Redwood-Demo".
Ethan Park: Okta arrived. HubSpot SSO kicks me back with "no seat". Notion 403. Drive says permission denied for Collateral.
Nima (Sales Ops): Assigning HubSpot seat now. Notion invite resent. Drive share applied. Tip: log out of SSO, clear cookies, then login again.
IT Helpdesk: Applied Drive perms. Gong license queued; onboarding email will come from support@gong.io. If you want manual access to recordings, tell us.
Ethan Park: HubSpot seat shows up after logout/login  good. Notion access works. Drive OK. Waiting on Gong email. Also curious: do we need VPN to demo Dedicated/private deployments to prospects?
Zoe Martinez: Short answer: no for sandbox demos. For customer-hosted private demos we coordinate with infra and use a secure tunnel/VPN per their policy. For sales conversations, mention we support Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.
IT Helpdesk: Gong license applied  onboarding email sent. If it doesn't arrive, check Promotions tab or run:
```
# verify user in Gong
gong-cli users:get --email ethan.park@redwood.com
```
Ethan Park: Got the Gong invite. Please enable transcripts and call highlights. Also where are the objection-handling playcards?
Nima (Sales Ops): Playcards: Notion > Sales Playbook > 'Objection Responses' and Drive -> Sales -> Playbooks -> Objection_Cards_2026.pdf. I'll drop a pinned link in this thread.
Access Bot: Update: HubSpot: done; Notion: done; Drive: done; Gong: done. Ticket #SALES-7001 closed.
Ethan Park: Everything's working now. Thanks for the quick help! One last ask: any recommended 1st-week shadow calls I should join?
Zoe Martinez: Join @CS-Standups Tue/Thu and @Sales-Enablement "Live Calls" sessions. I'll add you to the calendar invites. Welcome aboard :rocket:

source.messages:
Priya Natarajan: @here Hey team — Aurora Health POC just asked for a compliance bundle: SOC 2 Type II report, signed pen-test attestation letter, and a few architecture artifacts. They want something they can hand to their internal auditors by next Thursday. Can we do a trimmed set first?
Samir Patel (infosec): Which exact docs do they need for the POC? Full SOC2 report or our SOC2 bridge letter + description of scoping?
Priya Natarajan: They explicitly said 'SOC 2 Type II report or equivalent evidence.'
Elena Gomez (solutions-eng): For timelines, we can drop a minimal package: 1) SOC2 cover/summary (redacted) 2) control matrix 3) network diagram 4) key management overview 5) signed pen-test letter template filled. That should be deliverable in 3 business days if legal + infosec sign off.
Tom Wu (legal): I can draft the pen-test attestation. Need to confirm whether Aurora requires vendor sign-off wording or specific language. Priya, can you share the exact wording from their audit request?
Priya Natarajan: Pasted from their email: "Please provide SOC 2 Type II or equivalent, a signed penetration test attestation confirming no critical findings in last 12 months, and a short architecture diagram showing where data at-rest is encrypted."
Samir Patel (infosec): OK. We need to confirm two things: 1) Are they okay with a redacted SOC2 (we redact customer names and specific org identifiers)? 2) Do they accept our internal pen-test attestation or require an external 3rd-party pentest report?
Priya Natarajan: They said "equivalent" — follow-up said external report preferred but attestation acceptable for POC. I'll ask them to accept redacted copies for initial review.
Elena Gomez (solutions-eng): To speed, I'll assemble the architecture diagram and keyflow. Where should I drop artifacts? We have a compliance bucket but need an access path with limited TTL.
compliance-bot: Auto-note: Standard evidence upload path: s3://redwood-compliance/pending/aurora-health/ — requesters with temporary creds only. See https://redwood.example.com/internal/compliance-guide for steps.
Tom Wu (legal): Drafting pen-test attestation now. I'll include phrasing: "Redwood Inference attests that, to the best of its knowledge, no critical or high severity findings affecting core customer data confidentiality were unresolved as of [date]." Will circulate for Samir's sign-off.
Samir Patel (infosec): Once Tom posts draft, I'll add technical specifics about scope (in-scope services, timeframe, tester name), and sign off if wording aligns with our policy. Also confirm KMS/HSM language for Aurora's auditors.
Priya Natarajan: Procurement deadline is end of next week — if we can get minimal bundle by Wed EOD, we stay on track for the POC kickoff. Can we commit to Wed?
Elena Gomez (solutions-eng): Commit to delivering diagram + keyflow + control matrix by Wed EOD. Need legal + infosec to complete pen-test attestation + redacted SOC by Wed as well — can we prioritize? :pray:
Tom Wu (legal): I'll aim to have the pen-test attestation draft in your review by tomorrow morning.
Samir Patel (infosec): I'll review and provide sign-off by tomorrow afternoon. If they want full external pentest report later, we can schedule delivery but that may take longer.
Priya Natarajan: Great — I'll tell Aurora we can deliver a redacted SOC2 + signed attestation + architecture/keyflow by Wed and ask them to accept that for immediate audit intake. Thanks everyone. :thumbsup:
(compliance-bot): Evidence ticket created: COMP-2026-4321 for Aurora Health. Assigned: legal, infosec, solutions-eng. Reminder: use redaction checklist before upload.

source.messages:
Aisha Patel: Heads-up — Synthesis (security startup) signed for a POC. They want to run a custom-tokenizer + 8-bit quant variant of 'Sentinel-13B'. Can we do a quick catalog/compat check (KV cache, latency SLO, private deploy + KMS) so sales can lock the scope?

Ben Carter: Quick q — traffic shape? bursty chat or steady API? avg prompt/response sizes?

Aisha Patel: Chat assistant, avg ~120 tokens per user message, responses ~60-200 tokens. Expect bursts up to ~2k concurrent users during demos. They want p99 <250ms for short replies and private deployment (VPC) with customer-managed KMS.

Devon Zhang: Sentinel-13B not currently in catalog, but architecture looks Llama-ish. We can onboard as a variant. Notes: 8-bit quant + custom tokenizer is doable, but tokenizer adapter adds ~20-35ms preproc. KV cache works, but need to confirm cache key mapping with custom tokenizer. Also need to validate logit alignment after quant.

Priya Rao: For pricing — for Private/Dedicated I'd recommend reserving 3 nodes (A100-equivalent) for predictable perf. Back-of-envelope monthly OPEX ~ $28k-40k depending on region/commit. I'll paste the pricing template after. Link: https://redwood.internal/pricing/poc-templates (internal)

Aisha Patel: They also sent a security questionnaire. Any red flags for KMS/HSM or data residency? They require SOC2 + encryption at rest with CMK.

Devon Zhang: No blockers on infra side. Private control plane supports customer-managed KMS; we can route keys to their KMS. HSM integrations require legal and ops sign-off but are supported on request. We'll need to provide an SOC2 addendum; legal already flagged this.

InfraBot: synth-poc-sentinel: capacity template created in us-west2. CI perf-check job queued (job id: 98432).

Ben Carter: Action list: 1) Onboard variant (Devon) 2) Perf sweep with their tokenizer (Devon) 3) Security questionnaire -> legal (Aisha) 4) Pricing draft + committed nodes (Priya). Anyone disagree?

Devon Zhang: I'll kick off tokenizer perf tests — need ~2k utterances (varied lengths). @Aisha can you upload sample corpus and confirm any prompt-wrappers they use? Also confirm whether they accept pre-tokenize step or want server-side tokenization.

Aisha Patel: Uploading sample now (presigned S3 link — internal). They use a thin prompt wrapper; pasting snippet below:

```
model_variant: sentinel-13b-8bit
tokenizer: sentinel-custom-v1
batching: auto
kv_cache: enabled
latency_target_p99_ms: 250
```

Priya Rao: Draft pricing sheet shared to the thread (internal spreadsheet). Short summary: Hosted trial credits for initial week, then Private Reserved 3-node pack at ~$32k/mo with 12mo commit. Will refine with exact region multipliers.

Ben Carter: Cool. Risk level: medium until we validate tokenization/perf. Devon, please flag any accuracy regressions after quant.

Devon Zhang: Will do. I'll post perf numbers + any decoding mismatches by EOD tomorrow. If quant introduces >1.5% perplexity delta on their eval set we'll look at 4-bit fallback or mixed precision.

Aisha Patel: Great — looping legal on SOC2 addendum and Synthesis security contact. Thanks everyone :thumbsup:
